is a style of [Barbadian](/wiki/Barbados "Barbados") popular music created by [Jackie Opel](/wiki/Jackie_Opel "Jackie Opel") in the 1960s. It is primarily a fusion of Jamaican [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") with Trinidadian [calypso](/wiki/Calypso_music "Calypso music"), but is also influenced by a wide variety of musics from the British Isles and United States, including [sea shanties](/wiki/Sea_shanty "Sea shanty"), hymns, and [spirituals](/wiki/Spiritual_(music) "Spiritual (music)"). Spouge instrumentation originally consisted of [cowbell](/wiki/Cowbell_(instrument) "Cowbell (instrument)"), bass guitar, trap set, and various other electronic and percussion instruments, later augmented by [saxophone](/wiki/Saxophone "Saxophone"), [trombone](/wiki/Trombone "Trombone"), and [trumpets](/wiki/Trumpet "Trumpet").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Of these, the cowbell and the guitar are widely seen as the most integral part of the instrumentation, and are said to reflect the African origin of much of Barbadian music.

Millington, pg. 820 *Millington lists the American and British influences as including Welsh, Scottish and Irish elements, "transmitted through literature and poetry (Shakespeare and Milton), rhymes, folk songs, sea shanties, classical music, hymns, and other songs of praise (all of which have) been constantly available, providing entertainment, edification and general education to all people of Barbados. North American love songs, parlor songs, African-American spirituals and folk hymns, and hillbilly music have also contributed to a cultural mixture in which the love of a song, the expression through movement, and demand for theater continue to be of paramount importance".*

first_paragraph: **Spouge** is a style of [Barbadian](/wiki/Barbados "Barbados") popular music created by [Jackie Opel](/wiki/Jackie_Opel "Jackie Opel") in the 1960s. It is primarily a fusion of Jamaican [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") with Trinidadian [calypso](/wiki/Calypso_music "Calypso music"), but is also influenced by a wide variety of musics from the British Isles and United States, including [sea shanties](/wiki/Sea_shanty "Sea shanty"), hymns, and [spirituals](/wiki/Spiritual_(music) "Spiritual (music)"). Spouge instrumentation originally consisted of [cowbell](/wiki/Cowbell_(instrument) "Cowbell (instrument)"), bass guitar, trap set, and various other electronic and percussion instruments, later augmented by [saxophone](/wiki/Saxophone "Saxophone"), [trombone](/wiki/Trombone "Trombone"), and [trumpets](/wiki/Trumpet "Trumpet").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Of these, the cowbell and the guitar are widely seen as the most integral part of the instrumentation, and are said to reflect the African origin of much of Barbadian music.

**Spouge** is a style of [Barbadian](/wiki/Barbados "Barbados") popular music created by [Jackie Opel](/wiki/Jackie_Opel "Jackie Opel") in the 1960s. It is primarily a fusion of Jamaican [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") with Trinidadian [calypso](/wiki/Calypso_music "Calypso music"), but is also influenced by a wide variety of musics from the British Isles and United States, including [sea shanties](/wiki/Sea_shanty "Sea shanty"), hymns, and [spirituals](/wiki/Spiritual_(music) "Spiritual (music)"). Spouge instrumentation originally consisted of [cowbell](/wiki/Cowbell_(instrument) "Cowbell (instrument)"), bass guitar, trap set, and various other electronic and percussion instruments, later augmented by [saxophone](/wiki/Saxophone "Saxophone"), [trombone](/wiki/Trombone "Trombone"), and [trumpets](/wiki/Trumpet "Trumpet").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Of these, the cowbell and the guitar are widely seen as the most integral part of the instrumentation, and are said to reflect the African origin of much of Barbadian music.

Two different kinds of spouge were popular in the 1960s, *raw spouge* (*Draytons Two style*) and *dragon spouge* (*Cassius Clay style*). The spouge industry grew immensely by the end of the 1970s, and produced popular stars such as [The Escorts International](/w/index.php?title=The_Escorts_International&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Escorts International (page does not exist)"), [Blue Rhythm Combo](/w/index.php?title=Blue_Rhythm_Combo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Blue Rhythm Combo (page does not exist)"), the [Draytons Two](/wiki/Draytons_Two "Draytons Two"), [The Troubadours](/w/index.php?title=The_Troubadours_(Barbados)&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Troubadours (Barbados) (page does not exist)"), and [Desmond Weekes](/w/index.php?title=Desmond_Weekes&action=edit&redlink=1 "Desmond Weekes (page does not exist)"). Desmond Weekes, the former lead singer of the Draytons Two, claims the 1973 album *[Raw Spouge](/w/index.php?title=Raw_Spouge&action=edit&redlink=1 "Raw Spouge (page does not exist)")* to be "the only 100 per cent spouge album ever produced". The album topped the charts in a number of islands, including [St. Kitts](/wiki/St._Kitts "St. Kitts"), [St. Lucia](/wiki/St._Lucia "St. Lucia"), and [Dominica](/wiki/Dominica "Dominica").

In 1950, Opel sang with a band at Coconut Creek Club, St. James, and his Jackie Wilson like voice soon made him popular. Opel appeared as a supporting act on some local shows with well-known overseas performers, and usually became the star performer as he vigorously performed every note and "out shone" the star. During this time, ska, the forerunner beat to reggae, was popular in Jamaica, and calypso was popular in Trinidad. Jackie Opel and his band The Troubadours developed the spouge beat as Barbados' answer to ska in Jamaica and calypso in Trinidad. Spouge became so popular that every local band and singer in Barbados and throughout the Caribbean recorded their music using the spouge beat.

After six years, the art form declined. Today, very little spouge is played on the airwaves. Spouge is only played on Jackie Opel Day, Independence Day, Heroes Day, and Errol Barrow Day. Spouge has also declined for other reasons:

1. The man who created spouge, Jackie Opel, died in a car accident on March 9, 1970 at the age of 32.
2. The Barbadian media does not promote spouge to the extent that it can.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2002, Caribbean Records Inc. released a CD entitled *Vintage Spouge* with hits on it such as "Gimme Music" by Mike Grosvenor, "Any Day Now" by Richard Stoute and a cover of [Sam Cooke](/wiki/Sam_Cooke "Sam Cooke")'s "[You Send Me](/wiki/You_Send_Me "You Send Me")", sung by spouge creator Jackie Opel.

In 2024 "Pure Spouge Gospel" EP album was released which features five original songs written and performed by Lana Spooner-Jack in the Barbadian-owned genre Spouge. The music for the album was composed by mixing and mastering engineer Jeffrey Y. Grosvenor at his studio "Edge Cliff", in [Gatineau, Quebec](/wiki/Gatineau,_Quebec "Gatineau, Quebec") who added his own creativity by infusing African rhythms and Latin patterns. Each track on the album carries its own unique blend. The track "Come and Buy (Isaiah 55)" has a Meringue pattern that was altered to fit the Spouge rhythm; "In the Same Boat" has a Senagalese mbalax pattern. The congas are playing a Guaguancó pattern in "Drop by Drop"; and in the "Christmas I Believe In" track, the Spouge and the Cuban Bomba rhythms are played alternatively on the drum set. Very interesting combination! Of note, the song "Singing Over Me" is most likely the first Spouge song to be ever composed in the 3/4 time signature.
